description: Daily, weekly, and annual wage data for occupations tied to Tier 1 migration triggers during the peak migration era. Covers farm laborer, sharecropper, coal miner, textile mill hand, domestic servant, railroad worker, meatpacking worker, auto worker, and steelworker. THE CRITICAL COMPARISON: Southern agricultural wages versus Northern industrial wages -- this is the economic engine of the Great Migration and must be surfaced in every narrative involving Black Southern ancestors moving North or West.
data_source: BLS Historical Statistics of the United States (Millennial Edition); Gavin Wright, Old South New South (1986); USDA Agricultural Statistics; Women's Bureau Bulletins; union contract records (UMW, UMWA, CIO/UAW, UPWA); Ford Motor Company archives; U.S. Steel annual reports; Grossman, Land of Hope (1989); Halpern, Down on the Killing Floor (1997).

entries.context: Southern farm wages had barely moved in 30 years. A Black farm laborer in Alabama earned roughly what his father had earned in the 1870s while Northern factory wages had doubled. Source: Wright, Old South New South, statistical appendix.

A sharecropping family working 30-40 acres of cotton might produce 8-15 bales grossing $300-$600, but after the landlord's half and furnishing debts at 25-60% interest, net income was often $100-$200 -- or nothing. Source: USDA cotton statistics; Woodman, New South New Law (1995).

U.S. Steel common laborers earned $1.65-$2.00/day; skilled rollers and puddlers earned $3.00-$5.00/day. The 12-hour day and 7-day week were standard. Annual income was high relative to farm work but the human cost was enormous -- injury rates exceeded 25% per year. Source: Brody, Steelworkers in America (1960).

Packinghouse workers earned roughly $0.17-$0.25/hour for 10-hour days. Upton Sinclair's The Jungle (1906) documented working conditions. Wages were 3-5x what a Southern farm laborer earned -- this was the comparison that drove migration decisions. Source: BLS Bulletin No. 77 (1908); Halpern, Down on the Killing Floor.

Piedmont mill wages were still 50-60% of New England rates. Child labor was pervasive: children as young as 6 worked as doffers and spinners for $0.10-$0.25/day. A family with 3 workers in the mill earned $10-$15/week combined. Source: NC and SC labor commission reports.

Total compensation including room and board was $6-$9/week. Black women migrating north increasingly filled domestic positions as European immigrant women moved into factory and clerical work. Source: Women's Bureau Bulletin No. 39 (1924).

Miners paid by the ton ($0.40-$0.80/ton depending on seam). Company scrip, company housing ($2-$4/month), and company store markups reduced real wages by 15-30%. A miner earning $3/day in script spent $3.45 buying the same goods his cash-wage counterpart bought for $3. Source: WV and KY mine inspector reports.

The boll weevil arrived in Mississippi in 1907 and spread east, devastating cotton yields and reducing already meager farm incomes. The weevil was as much a push factor for the Great Migration as Northern wages were a pull factor. Source: Giesen, Boll Weevil Blues (2011).

WWI cotton demand raised prices to $0.20-$0.35/lb (1916-1919), briefly improving sharecropper incomes. But the boll weevil, floods, and the postwar price crash to $0.10/lb by 1920 wiped out gains. Source: USDA Agricultural Statistics.

THE WATERSHED MOMENT. Henry Ford's $5 day doubled the prevailing factory wage overnight. Before January 1914, Ford paid $2.34/day. The announcement drew 10,000 job seekers to Highland Park on January 12. A sharecropper clearing $200/year in Mississippi could earn $1,300 at Ford -- more than six times as much. This single data point reshaped American migration patterns. Source: Meyer, The Five Dollar Day (1981).

Chicago stockyards employed over 40,000 workers by 1917. Black workers recruited from the South during WWI labor shortages earned $0.25-$0.35/hour -- 4-6x Southern farm wages. The Chicago Defender ran ads: 'Come North, Where There Is More Humanity and More Money.' Source: Grossman, Land of Hope; Halpern, Down on the Killing Floor.

WWI demand pushed steel production and wages higher. U.S. Steel common laborers earned $2.50-$3.00/day by 1918. Skilled positions paid $4-$6/day. The 1919 steel strike sought the 8-hour day and union recognition. Source: Brody, Steelworkers in America; Interchurch World Movement report (1920).

WWI labor shortages raised domestic wages. Black women from the South earned $5-$8/week in Northern cities vs. $1.50-$3.00/week in the South. The wage gap was smaller than for men but still significant. Source: Women's Bureau Bulletin No. 39.

Agricultural depression hit Southern farmers hard through the 1920s even while the rest of the country boomed. Cotton fell to $0.12-$0.20/lb. The gap between Southern farm wages and Northern factory wages widened to its greatest extent. Source: Wright, Old South New South.

USDA studies in the 1920s found the average Black sharecropper family in the Cotton Belt netted $200-$350/year. Compare this to a Chicago meatpacking worker earning $25-$30/week ($1,300-$1,560/year). The Northern worker earned in one month what the sharecropper earned in a year. This is the comparison the narrative AI must surface. Source: USDA Farmers' Bulletin; Woofter, Landlord and Tenant on the Cotton Plantation (1936).

Ford raised to $6/day in 1919, $7/day in 1929. Other automakers followed. A Detroit auto worker earned 6-10x what a Mississippi sharecropper earned. But Black workers were largely confined to the foundry and paint departments -- the hottest, most dangerous jobs. Source: Babson, Working Detroit (1984).

Packinghouse wages rose through the 1920s but remained below auto and steel. Workers earned $0.40-$0.60/hour. The work was grueling and injury rates high, but the income dwarfed Southern agricultural pay. Source: Halpern, Down on the Killing Floor.
After the failed 1919 strike, U.S. Steel grudgingly adopted the 8-hour day in 1923. Wages for common labor rose to $4-$5/day; skilled workers earned $6-$8/day. Source: U.S. Steel annual reports.

The 1920s coal industry was in chronic depression. Overproduction meant short work weeks (3-4 days common). UMW miners in union fields earned $7.50/day but worked only 150-200 days. Non-union Southern Appalachian miners earned $3-$4/day. Source: Corbin, Life Work and Rebellion in the Coal Fields (1981).

Cotton fell to $0.05-$0.06/lb in 1931-1932. Farm wages collapsed. Many landlords simply stopped paying wages and offered only food and shelter. AAA crop reduction payments (1933+) went to landlords, not tenants, and many sharecroppers were evicted. Source: Grubbs, Cry from the Cotton (1971).

THE KEY STATISTIC FOR GREAT MIGRATION NARRATIVES. The USDA 1934 survey found average Black sharecropper family income was $295/year in the Cotton Belt. Meanwhile, a Northern factory worker still employed during the Depression earned $800-$1,200/year. Even in the worst economic crisis in American history, the North-South wage gap persisted. Source: USDA, Economic Status of Negroes (1935); Woofter, Landlord and Tenant.

Detroit auto employment fell from 475,000 in 1929 to 125,000 in 1933. Those still working earned good daily wages but worked only 2-4 days/week. The UAW sit-down strikes of 1936-1937 won union recognition at GM and Chrysler. Source: Fine, Sit-Down (1969).

Packinghouse employment fell 30-40% during the Depression. Those still working saw hours cut. The Packinghouse Workers Organizing Committee (CIO) began organizing in 1937, eventually winning higher wages and better conditions. Source: Halpern, Down on the Killing Floor.

The CCC employed 3 million young men 1933-1942. Pay was $30/month with $25 compulsorily sent home. For many Southern Black and white families, the $25 monthly allotment was more reliable income than farming. Room, board, clothing, and medical care were provided. Source: Salmond, The Civilian Conservation Corps (1967).

WWII labor shortages finally pushed Southern farm wages up significantly. But the gap with Northern industry remained enormous. A Mississippi farm hand earning $2/day ($500/year) could earn $8-$12/day in a Detroit defense plant ($2,000-$3,000/year). The Second Great Migration accelerated. Source: Wright, Old South New South; USDA farm wage reports.

WWII cotton prices rose to $0.20-$0.22/lb, improving incomes temporarily. But mechanization (the mechanical cotton picker, perfected 1944) was beginning to eliminate the need for sharecropper labor entirely. The system that had trapped millions was dissolving -- not through justice but through machines. Source: Holley, The Second Great Emancipation (2000).

Defense plant wages represented the highest industrial wages Black workers had ever earned. The UAW contract at Ford (1941) set base pay at $1.15/hour; overtime and shift premiums pushed take-home higher. A Black worker from Mississippi earning $3,000/year in 1943 was earning 10x his sharecropper father's income. Source: Meier & Rudwick, Black Detroit and the Rise of the UAW (1979).

UPWA (United Packinghouse Workers of America) contracts raised wages to $0.85-$1.25/hour by the late 1940s. The union was notably interracial, with Black workers in leadership positions. Meatpacking remained a critical entry point for Southern Black migrants. Source: Halpern, Down on the Killing Floor.

USWA (United Steelworkers of America) contracts provided the highest blue-collar wages in America. Pittsburgh, Gary, Cleveland, and Birmingham steelworkers earned $1.00-$1.50/hour base with overtime premiums. A steelworker's annual income exceeded what most college-educated Black professionals earned in the South. Source: USWA contract records.

John L. Lewis and the UMW won major wage increases during WWII. Miners earned $1.00-$1.25/hour by 1945. But mechanization was eliminating jobs: Appalachian coal employment fell from 700,000 in 1923 to 400,000 by 1950 and would plummet further. Displaced miners became migrants themselves. Source: Eller, Uneven Ground (2008).

Southern textile wages finally rose during WWII but remained 30-40% below Northern industrial averages. The Fair Labor Standards Act (1938) had begun closing the gap by setting minimum wages, but Southern textile remained a low-wage industry. Source: Hall et al., Like a Family; BLS wage data.

West Coast shipyards (Kaiser, Marinship) paid premium wages that drew massive migration from the South, Southwest, and Dust Bowl states. Richmond CA grew from 23,000 to 100,000 during the war. Black workers from Louisiana, Texas, and Arkansas earned more in a month than they had in a year. Source: Johnson, The Second Gold Rush (1993).

Southern farm wages finally rose substantially in the postwar era as mechanization reduced labor demand and out-migration shrank the labor supply. But the North-South industrial wage gap remained 30-50%. The economic logic of migration persisted. Source: USDA Agricultural Statistics; Wright, Old South New South.

Black domestic servants in the South earned half what their Northern counterparts earned. A cook or maid in Atlanta or Memphis earned $1.50-$2.50/week; the same work in New York or Philadelphia paid $4.00-$6.00/week. Source: Hunter, To 'Joy My Freedom (1997).

By the 1930s-1940s, live-in service was declining in favor of day work paid by the hour ($0.25-$0.50/hour). Black women who had earned $2/week in the South earned $10-$20/week in Northern cities. This wage gap drove female migration independently of male employment patterns. Source: Clark-Lewis, Living In Living Out (1994).

Sweatshop garment workers in NYC's Lower East Side earned piece rates that varied wildly by skill and speed. A skilled operator could earn $7/week; a new immigrant learner might earn $3. The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire of 1911 exposed conditions. Source: Women's Bureau reports; Howe, World of Our Fathers (1976).

The ILGWU's 1909 'Uprising of the 20,000' and subsequent strikes improved wages and conditions. Protocol of Peace (1910) established collective bargaining in the cloak trade. Source: Levine, The Women's Garment Workers (1924).

cost_of_living_benchmarks.context: Tenement rent consumed 25-35% of a laborer's income. A family earning $500/year spent $100-$150 on rent, leaving $350-$400 for everything else for a family of 5-7. Source: BLS Cost of Living surveys.
Flour prices were at historic lows due to Great Plains wheat production. A family consumed 25 lbs every 2-3 weeks.
Coffee was a daily necessity for working families. A household used 1-2 lbs per week.

Urban families consumed 4-8 tons per winter. The 1902 anthracite strike caused a national coal shortage and pushed prices to $10-$14/ton, forcing families to burn wood furniture. Source: Cornell, The Anthracite Coal Strike of 1902 (1957).
Before electrification, kerosene was the primary lighting fuel. A family used 1-2 gallons per week for lamps.

The Model T went from a luxury item costing nearly two years of a sharecropper's income (1908) to something an industrial worker could buy with 2-3 months' wages (1925). This revolution in personal transportation changed migration patterns: families could drive North instead of taking the train. Source: Ford Motor Company price lists.

cost_of_living_benchmarks.item: Train ticket, Greenwood MS to Chicago (one way)
cost_of_living_benchmarks.context: A one-way ticket from the Mississippi Delta to Chicago cost roughly two weeks' wages picking cotton but less than one week's wages at a Chicago meatpacking plant. Labor agents sometimes advanced the fare, deducting it from first paychecks. The Illinois Central Railroad was the main artery of the Great Migration. Source: ICC rate records; Grossman, Land of Hope.

Newly arrived Black migrants were confined to the South Side 'Black Belt' where rents were 25-50% higher than comparable white neighborhoods due to housing discrimination. A kitchenette apartment (one room with shared bathroom) cost $8-$12/month. Source: Drake & Cayton, Black Metropolis (1945).
The nickel streetcar fare was universal in American cities. A worker commuting daily spent $0.50-$0.60/week on transit -- 2-3% of weekly wages.

Rents rose through the 1920s. A steelworker or auto worker earning $30-$40/week spent $15-$25/month on rent (10-15% of income). This was a dramatic improvement over the 30-40% rent burden of the previous generation.

Radios went from novelty to necessity in the 1920s. A basic crystal set cost $25; a tube radio with speaker cost $60-$100. For a factory worker earning $30/week, a radio cost 1-3 weeks' wages. Installment plans made ownership possible. Source: Sears Roebuck catalog.
Movies were the primary entertainment for working-class families. A nickelodeon cost $0.05-$0.10; a picture palace charged $0.15-$0.50. A family outing cost $0.50-$1.00.

cost_of_living_benchmarks.cost: $1.00-$3.00 (urban only; most rural South was unelectrified until TVA)
cost_of_living_benchmarks.context: Only 10% of Southern farms had electricity in 1930 vs. 85% of urban homes. Rural electrification would not reach most of the South until the late 1930s-1940s through REA/TVA. Source: Kitchens & Fishback, Flip the Switch (2015).

cost_of_living_benchmarks.item: Train ticket, Mississippi to Chicago (one way)
cost_of_living_benchmarks.context: Despite Depression-era deflation, train fares held relatively steady. For a sharecropper family clearing $295/year (USDA 1934 average), two tickets North cost $17-$22 -- nearly a month's net income. Many migrants sold everything they owned to buy passage. Source: IC Railroad rate tables.
Depression deflation brought food prices to historic lows, but wages fell faster. Relative to income, food was more expensive during the Depression than during prosperity.

Coal company houses were crude: 3-4 rooms, no plumbing, no insulation. Rent was deducted before the miner saw a paycheck. Eviction followed termination within days. Source: Eller, Miners Millhands and Mountaineers.
Wartime rent controls kept rents from rising as fast as wages. For the first time, many working-class families spent less than 20% of income on rent. Source: OPA rent control records.
Wartime rationing limited availability of meat, sugar, and butter. Eggs were not rationed and became a dietary staple.
Home milk delivery was standard. A family with children consumed 1-2 quarts daily. Monthly milk bill: $4-$10.

cost_of_living_benchmarks.item: Bus/train ticket, Alabama to Detroit (one way)
cost_of_living_benchmarks.context: By the 1940s, Greyhound buses offered cheaper alternatives to trains. A bus from Birmingham to Detroit cost $11-$13. For a defense plant worker earning $60/week, the fare was less than two days' pay. The economic barrier to migration had effectively disappeared. Source: Greyhound rate tables; Wilkerson, The Warmth of Other Suns (2010).

cost_of_living_benchmarks.item: Train ticket, Louisiana to Los Angeles (one way)
cost_of_living_benchmarks.context: The Southern Pacific coach fare from New Orleans to Los Angeles was roughly $30. For a shipyard worker about to earn $60-$80/week, this was 3-4 days' pay. Thousands of Black families from Louisiana and Texas made this journey during WWII. Source: SP Railroad rate tables.
